headline: Doha hosts indirect US-Iran talks
deck: Qatar and Pakistan are mediating technical sessions on the mid-June memorandum while both capitals insist the channel stays indirect. Twenty-one South Korean-operated vessels have crossed Hormuz safely since the ceasefire as Brent trades near $72. IAEA chief Rafael Grossi says inspections will proceed once modalities are settled.

art.title: The strait quiets as the talks stay indirect
art.caption: US-Iran technical talks run through mediators in Doha as Hormuz traffic normalises and the war premium drains · Map: Sprockett, Escalation Desk · Terrain: NOAA ETOPO1

body: US and Iranian technical delegations convened indirect talks in Doha on 1 July, with Reuters confirming the sessions through a source with direct knowledge and an Iranian official [E1]. Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner were not attending those technical discussions themselves, keeping Washington's senior envoys outside the working-room sessions Qatar and Pakistan mediated [E1].

Qatar's foreign ministry said no high-level US-Iran meetings were taking place under the agreed mechanism [E2]. US envoys met Qatari intermediaries instead, while Iranian and American technical teams remained in session, directly and indirectly, with those mediators [E2].

Prime Minister Sheikh Mohammed bin Abdulrahman Al-Thani received Witkoff and Kushner on 30 June as Doha sustained its broker role [E3]. The ministry said the agenda still covered Hormuz shipping, the nuclear file, frozen Iranian funds, and Lebanon alongside broader regional questions [E2].

President Trump told reporters both governments were “getting along very well” and repeated his claim that Iran had requested a meeting in Doha [E4][E5]. Iranian officials denied that any meeting at any level with the American side had been scheduled, a denial Qatar's public statements aligned with [E5][E6].

Working groups following up on the mid-June memorandum kept the process alive without delivering a settlement [E1]. Pakistan joined Qatar in hosting the channel Tehran has kept indirect, leaving passage rules, nuclear safeguards, and fund releases on the table without resolution [E1][E2].

South Korea's oceans ministry said 21 South Korean-operated vessels had passed safely through the strait since the ceasefire was signed two weeks earlier [E7]. The damaged bulk carrier Namu was due to leave Hormuz in mid-July once repairs finished, a timeline Reuters framed as part of a wider shipping normalisation [E7].

Reuters recorded no cross-verified report of fresh US-Iran strikes or new Hormuz tanker casualties in the past day as the mutual stand-down held into 1 July [E1]. That kinetic quiet coincided with Brent crude trading near $72 per barrel as oil shed much of its Gulf war premium [E8].

IAEA Director General Rafael Grossi said on 24 June that inspections “will go ahead” while the agency worked out modalities with Tehran [E9]. Through 1 July, no official confirmation placed inspectors back inside the most sensitive affected sites, leaving access agreed in principle but not yet restored [E9].

key_numbers.label: South Korean-operated vessels through Hormuz since ceasefire
IAEA inspectors back at struck sites

evidence_box.fragment: technical talks were underway in Doha
no high-level U.S.-Iran meetings were taking place
Iran had requested a meeting in Doha
denied that any meeting at any level
21 South Korean-operated vessels had passed safely
